little_template_justification: The introduction is anchored by a substantive puzzle: why caste-based social segregation persists and whether welfare can weaken coethnic dependence and improve intergroup ties. It also frames the paper as filling a gap by positioning welfare as an underexplored driver of caste change, rather than mainly adjudicating a live debate between competing camps.
intro_presents_main_result_quote: Consistent with my hypotheses, where welfare reduces in-group economic reliance, I find reduced social investment in co-ethnics and improved ties with non-coethnics. By contrast, where land ownership is more concentrated in the hands of a dominant caste, RBS benefits are less likely to reach a broad cross-section of castes. In these areas, I do not find significant effects on borrowing from caste members or intercaste ties.
intro_prior_update_quote: In these villages, welfare reduced borrowing from caste members by over 40%. It also increased meal sharing, lowered the likelihood that most or all of respondents’ friends were of the same caste by 33%, and raised the donation amount by 19%, indicating that respondents were more willing to allocate resources toward an outgroup.
section_titles: THE IMPACT OF WELFARE ON CASTE-BASED SOCIAL INSURANCE
THE IMPACT OF WELFARE ON CASTE-BASED SOCIAL INTEGRATION
HOW CASTE-BASED SOCIAL INSURANCE SHAPES SOCIAL INTEGRATION
